metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

model-identification asserts that the dashboard shows the model the agent was launched with — ModelName != 'unknown', and ContextWindow matching the model's published window — rather than 'unknown'. The state arc is the basic-turn arc (ready → working → ready); the extra intent over basic-turn is purely that the daemon extracted a model_name.

WHERE THE MODEL CLAIM LIVES: opencode records the active model in its SQLite store under message.data.model = {providerID, modelID}. The opencode metrics path (metrics.go:117-131) reads modelID (fallback top-level modelID), runs it through tailer.NormalizeModelName, and sets SessionMetrics.ModelName; ComputeContextUtilization then derives ContextWindow/ContextWindowUnknown from that model via the capacity manager (metrics.go:255-256). The launch model can be set with `opencode run --model <provider/model>`, but the driver passes NO --model flag, so opencode boots on its configured default; the scenario is about the daemon extracting that name, not about which model produced the tokens. Session state lives in opencode's SQLite store (~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db), observed by the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore source as the UUID-keyed ses_… session for the cwd — so daemon_capability: full.

The model_name assertion is NOT expressible in the events.jsonl phase DSL (the validator Phase struct carries no model field), so expected.jsonl covers only the basic-turn lifecycle skeleton and the model presence is asserted at summary.model_name in the .replay.json.golden (which replay-fixtures.sh diffs) plus the opencode metrics unit tests.

The interactive driver (drive-opencode-interactive.sh) launches a blocking `opencode run -- "<prompt>"` per send and chains --session for continuity; wait_turn is a no-op because the run already blocks. model-identification is a single plain-text turn (one send + wait_turn + trailing sleep) — well within this grammar, so driver_capability: ready.

MODEL note: this worktree's opencode default is the local LM Studio model whose modelID normalizes to 'gemma-4-e2b-it-uncensored' (set in ~/.config/opencode/opencode.json). It is small and slow (~30-45s per turn), so the recipe budgets a generous timeout (180s) and a trailing sleep that outlasts the turn_done settle. The observed/reported ModelName is the configured default model (not a Claude/Anthropic model); the assertion is presence (non-empty, not 'unknown'), with the concrete string being that default.
caveats: OpenCode does not expose CLI-assignable session IDs; the driver discovers the session id by querying SQLite for the most recent row keyed on directory after the first `opencode run` returns.
The local gemma model is slow (~30-45s per turn); the recipe uses a 180s timeout and a generous trailing sleep so a slow turn does not trip a false timeout or miss the working→ready settle.

ContextWindow resolves to the model's published window only when the model is in the capacity table; for a model the table does not cover, ModelName is still correct but ContextWindow reads 0 / ContextWindowUnknown=true. The model assertion is presence (non-empty, not 'unknown') per issue #440, not a hardcoded string.

sources.note: model["modelID"] (fallback top-level modelID) → tailer.NormalizeModelName → metrics.ModelName; ComputeContextUtilization(metrics.ModelName, ...) derives ContextWindow/ContextWindowUnknown. raw["_model"] carries the raw modelID into the event.
Source variant ProcessOwnedStore (SQLite at ~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db) with ExactName{Name: "opencode"} process matcher; daemon polls the session table for the ses_… session.

Sibling recording: a single `opencode run` emits state_transition new_state ready→working→ready on one ses_… session — the same arc this cell records, with the spec framing the model-extraction intent.
`--model | -m` model specification; the driver omits it so opencode uses its configured default model.

preconditions: opencode CLI on PATH and authenticated against its default model (run a single message in the opencode TUI to confirm; the worktree default is a local LM Studio model so no remote key is needed)
irrlichd is recording with the opencode SQLite watcher enabled — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

the configured default model in ~/.config/opencode/opencode.json is the one whose name the dashboard should report; the driver passes NO --model flag, so whatever default is set is what gets asserted as ModelName (not 'unknown')
setup: The interactive driver (drive-opencode-interactive.sh) creates a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/ and launches a blocking `opencode run -- "<prompt>"` for the single send (no --model flag, so opencode uses its configured default model); the run blocks until the assistant turn finishes, so wait_turn is a no-op

OpenCode session state lives in its SQLite store (~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db), not per-cwd transcript files; the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore source polls the session table and observes the UUID-keyed ses_… session for this cwd. The driver discovers that session id from SQLite after the run returns (opencode does not expose CLI-assignable session IDs)

opencode records the active model on each message under message.data.model = {providerID, modelID}; the metrics path reads modelID (fallback top-level modelID) → tailer.NormalizeModelName → SessionMetrics.ModelName, and ComputeContextUtilization derives ContextWindow from that model. The scenario is about the daemon extracting that name, not about which model produced the tokens

The send is a no-tool single turn — opencode is asked to reply with exactly one word, so the working span carries no auto-executed tool call (tool_calls_max: 0) and the session never enters waiting (no permission gate fires)

ModelName/ContextWindow are checked AFTER turn_done — ContextWindow may briefly read 0 between session detection and the first assistant event (per assessment caveat); the default model is a small, slow local LM Studio model (~30–45s per turn), so the trailing sleep and 180s timeout are generous so a slow turn must not trip a false timeout and the trailing sleep must outlast the turn_done settle so working→ready is observed

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok

verify: events.jsonl contains a transcript_new (or session_created) for the ses_… session, born ready, within ~2s of agent launch (a transient proc-<PID> pre-session may also appear; the turn arc and pid_discovered land on the ses_… session)
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the ses_… session_id
events.jsonl shows one ready→working→ready cycle on the ses_… session_id (the single assistant turn); no state_transition to waiting appears (no tool call, no permission gate)

the .replay.json.golden carries a non-empty summary.model_name (NOT 'unknown') — opencode's metrics path pulled it from message.data.model.modelID and normalized it. This is where model identification is asserted, since the events.jsonl phase DSL has no model field. The concrete value follows the configured default (this worktree: the local LM Studio model normalizing to 'gemma-4-e2b-it-uncensored'); assert presence + non-'unknown'
the session's final live state is ready
recipe.notes: Single no-op turn in one headless opencode session: captures that the dashboard shows the configured default model (ModelName != 'unknown') instead of 'unknown'. TEMPLATED FROM opencode/basic-turn (same single send + wait_turn + trailing sleep shape) — the only extra intent over basic-turn is that the daemon extracted a model_name. RECORDABILITY (no driver_gap): the recipe needs only send/wait_turn/sleep, all implemented by drive-opencode-interactive.sh. WHY a blocking `opencode run` (not a long-lived REPL with teardown, unlike codex/pi): opencode's driver launches a fresh blocking `opencode run` per send and chains --session; the run already blocks until the turn completes, so wait_turn is a no-op and there is no in-process REPL to tear down — hence no teardown/process_exited phase (the opencode sibling specs basic-turn and turn-end-terminal-text omit it for the same reason). WHERE THE MODEL CLAIM LIVES: opencode stores model = {providerID, modelID} in its SQLite store; metrics.go reads modelID → NormalizeModelName → SessionMetrics.ModelName, and ComputeContextUtilization derives ContextWindow. The model assertion is NOT expressible in the events.jsonl phase DSL (the validator Phase struct carries no model field), so expected.jsonl asserts only the basic-turn lifecycle skeleton (one ready→working→ready arc on one ses_… session, no waiting, final ready); the user-observable model signal is asserted at summary.model_name in the .replay.json.golden (which replay-fixtures.sh diffs) plus the opencode metrics unit tests. SESSION IDENTITY: session_birth is anchored to recording start UNPINNED so a transient proc-<PID> pre-session can't steal identity; turn1_start (matching working) establishes the ses_… session identity that turn1_done chains off via same_session_as. MODEL note: the driver passes NO --model flag, so whatever default is configured in ~/.config/opencode/opencode.json is reported — this worktree boots on a local LM Studio model whose modelID normalizes to 'gemma-4-e2b-it-uncensored'; per issue #440 the concrete model string is environment-dependent, so assert presence (non-empty, not 'unknown'), with the value following the configured default. What differs between runs: the concrete model value (follows the configured default), token counts/cost, ses_… UUID/PID/timestamps, assistant wording (pinned with 'Reply with exactly the word: ok'). What must NOT differ: one ready→working→ready arc on one ses_… session, a non-empty summary.model_name in the golden, and a final ready state. The local gemma model is small and slow (~30–45s per turn), so the trailing 6s sleep outlasts the turn_done settle and the 180s timeout absorbs a slow turn.
